_doc: Live-narration ADAPTATION cue sheet for The Unnecessary, Ch2 Scene 2 (Where Are the Machines). Lena's viewpoint, picking up the instant after Scene 1: Eli has arrived; he counts the three machines, gives Lena the truthful limits of his fix, and she shows him the dusty back-room server space and leaves him to it. Begins at this scene's first manuscript line ('Eli stood inside the door...') and ends at its last ('...toward the part that was hers'); does NOT recap Scene 1's door/'Lena' beat or pre-empt Scene 3. Two live voices in the room: eli_rook (clean, dry, grave) and lena_okafor (controlled, afraid under the steadiness). The narrator (narrator voice, calm, fixed tuning) carries Lena's third-person perception and interior verbatim to canon -- the canon-gold beats are kept (the man from Asterion she will neither absolve nor reduce; the machine that lies with total confidence and no malice; she only needed his hands). Cut tags the voices already carry ('he said'/'she said'); cut physical beats the SFX legitimately replace (the bag set down -> bag-set-down; the swollen door shouldered open -> swollen-door; the work light snapped on -> worklight-click). One remembered fragment dramatized: Eli's afternoon-link words 'Tell me what stops. Physically.' performed in his own voice through the book-scope degraded 'link' filter, framed by the narrator ('the link that afternoon had dropped his words into holes' / 'and the holes, and his voice coming back. Now there were no holes') -- the paragraph IS about that contrast, so we hear it. The man on the controller is deliberately UNNAMED (Lena keeps his name back; canon). 'text' = canon spelling; 'tts' = pronunciation-corrected line sent (present-tense read->reed; Asterion/Vesely/Reyes/Dembele phoneticized). Reveal-safe: Asterion is in-manuscript and kept; NO Morrow, no Mars/Aurelia, no six-years; the fix's OUTCOME stays unestablished (in-progress only), per blueprint. Voices (all on server): narrator, eli_rook, lena_okafor. Passed Gemini adaptation-critique (gemini-pro-latest).

filters._doc: No scene-special filters. 'link' (degraded live-call wobble with periodic dropouts) resolves up-scope from the book filters.json; used once on Eli's remembered afternoon-link fragment. 'none' is provided by the mixer.

tuning._doc: Narrator stays calm/measured regardless of scene intensity (over-expressive narration artifacts). Characters carry the scene's banked dread and dry steadiness. Eli: low, precise, grave -- delivering hard truths quietly, not performing them. Lena: controlled, demanding the truth not the comfort, fear held just under the surface. Per-cue overrides win; short clipped lines get lower temperature + higher cfg_weight to avoid garble.

cues.text: Eli stood inside the door with the cold still on him and a canvas bag in one hand, the three o'clock light gone dull and gray in the door glass behind him, and he looked, as he always did, like a man who had walked through the neighborhood reading it.

He was taller than she let herself remember between times, gray come further into the close-cut hair than a year ago. He did not say hello. It was the one habit they shared in a doorway. He looked past her shoulder down the hall toward the rooms, already counting.
Where are they.
Two in that room. Scanner and the cabinet, the medication unit. The controller's down the end, in with the man.
She did not say his name. She had not said it to Eli over the link that afternoon, and she did not say it now. She kept it back, a name she was not ready to set down on someone else's table.
Three machines. One of each. That's the whole list.
One scanner. One med unit. One controller.
He was fixing the count in place.
And the man needs the controller.
He's off it now, in the daylight. He comes off for hours, sits up, breathes on his own. But he can't hold a night. After dark it goes back on him and stays on till morning.

She watched him take the shape of it, three machines, one body, his eyes going a little distant in what she had learned to read as the part of him she both wanted and could not stand. He was already building it.
cues.tts: She watched him take the shape of it, three machines, one body, his eyes going a little distant in what she had learned to reed as the part of him she both wanted and could not stand. He was already building it.

cues.text: Eli. Before you go up into it.
He looked at her, and she made the most of having his eyes.
You have the order of it from the link, all three. The man first. Everything else second.
Understood.
Where's your server. You've got an old one. Every place like this has one, a closet, a room, something the last tenant left. I need to be near it and near power.
Down the back.
She went past him and he followed, and she felt the strangeness of it, his real footsteps behind her in the hall instead of his voice coming and going in a speaker.
The link that afternoon had dropped his words into holes.
Tell me what stops. Physically.
And the holes, and his voice coming back. Now there were no holes. He was here, which was supposed to help.
She made herself ask the question while she could still ask it walking, before he was bent over the rack and she had handed him the night.
Can you keep them running. The man, tonight. Yes or the truth, not the comfort.

He did not answer right away, which was its own answer, and she respected him for not filling it. They had been doing this for four years. His honesty and his competence had the same shape in him, the same root, and tonight she needed the one and dreaded the other.

I can make the machines stop waiting on the company. That part I can do. When a server goes quiet, you stand in for it. You put up something local that answers in its place, and the machine stops asking the sky and starts asking the closet, and it runs. I can do that for a machine. The controller, maybe, if the closet cooperates and I have the hours.
And then it's fine.
And then it runs.
He said it carefully, and she heard him choosing the words as carefully as he chose a connector, the right one, the one that went home.
You already know the rest. You said it back to me on the link this afternoon, before I could.
He handed it over rather than teach it again.

I can stand in for the yes. Not for what the yes was good for. That doesn't fit in a closet, and it won't fit before midnight. So the machine runs, and it believes itself when it tells you it's fine, and the only thing changed is who it answers to. Not the company anymore. Me, and a closet.

She had known this. He had reached for it that afternoon and stopped short, and she had filled in the rest herself in the hours since, in clinical words because the engineering was his and the body was hers. A machine she could not trust. A machine that would say a pressure was right because nothing was left to tell it the pressure was wrong.

She had spent her whole career arguing that a visible human deciding was safer than an automated one. Now she would have to lean on a system no one could see anymore, repaired by one man into something that would lie to her with total confidence and no malice at all.
How many can you do. Of the three. By midnight.
I don't know yet. One, well. Maybe two. Not all three the way I'd want any of them.
His jaw did not tighten. His face did none of what other people's faces did. He simply told her.
It's hours a machine, done right. I came to look before I said anything was impossible. So show me the room and let me look.
She showed him the room. The door had swelled in its frame and she had to put her shoulder to it.
The air that came out was the stale cold of a year unopened, dust and old electronics and something faintly sweet, mouse maybe, the smell of a place the world had stopped coming into.

The clinic's own server sat on a steel rack under a window painted shut, a squat machine with a single amber light, older than the scanner by a decade, underpowered, kept alive for the records and nothing harder. A work light hung off a hook. There was a stool.

Down the hall they had passed the food board, and his eyes had caught on it for half a second, the cork panel by the back door where Dembele pinned his columns, what the Vesely place had and what the Reyes place needed and who would carry which to whom. Then his eyes came off it, because it was not a machine, and went to the rack.
cues.tts: Down the hall they had passed the food board, and his eyes had caught on it for half a second, the cork panel by the back door where Dem-beh-leh pinned his columns, what the Vess-eh-lee place had and what the Ray-ess place needed and who would carry which to whom. Then his eyes came off it, because it was not a machine, and went to the rack.

cues.text: He crouched in front of the old server without being asked, as he crouched in front of everything, and his hands went to it, fast and certain, fluent in a language she did not speak. She felt the old pull, the relief of competence, and under it the old cold mistrust of where the competence went.

He could save the man. He could also be the man from Asterion, from the labs that had hollowed out half the city's work. She knew most of that history. She had decided years ago not to absolve it and not to reduce him to it, and tonight she had room for neither. Tonight she only needed his hands.
cues.tts: He could save the man. He could also be the man from As-teer-ee-on, from the labs that had hollowed out half the city's work. She knew most of that history. She had decided years ago not to absolve it and not to reduce him to it, and tonight she had room for neither. Tonight she only needed his hands.

cues.text: He spoke to the machine, not to her.

This'll hold a light and a board and not much. I'll have to be careful what I draw. Your power's already cut, you're on the low tier, no emergency restore. If the grid sags tonight and the batteries take it, I lose whatever I've got going and the room goes dark with the rest of you.
I know about the tier.
Everyone knew about the tier. The same clean type had dropped the whole neighborhood at noon, the clinic with it, no more emergencies, only standard maintenance windows whenever those came.
If the power goes, the controller's on its own backup for a while. Then it isn't.
Then I'll be quick.
It was not a promise. They both knew it, and neither of them dressed it as one.
She stood in the doorway a moment longer. He had already gone into it, the bag open, a coil of cable out.
The work light threw his bent shadow long across the painted-shut window.

There was nothing for her here. The work was his, and she could not do it, could not even watch it usefully; her watching might cost him the concentration that was the only thing in this room worth anything. The decisive work of the night would happen three feet from where she stood, and she could not lift a finger of it.

She pulled the door most of the way closed on him, on the dust and the single light and the bowed shoulders, left it a hand's width open, and turned back down the hall toward the people, toward the part that was hers.
cues.tts: She pulled the door most of the way closed on him, on the dust and the single light and the boud shoulders, left it a hand's width open, and turned back down the hall toward the people, toward the part that was hers.
